title: Numbers, NaN and precision

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Numbers, NaN and precision; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ECMAScript Language Specification; living specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Numbers, NaN and precision
sources.claim: JavaScript number uses IEEE-754 binary floating point, so safe integers and decimal fractions have finite precision limits.

canonicalExplanation.summary: JavaScript number uses IEEE-754 binary floating point, so safe integers and decimal fractions have finite precision limits.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: 0.1 + 0.2 is compared with a tolerance instead of exact equality to 0.3. Your goal is to implement and test numeric validation without relying on misleading NaN comparisons. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Scripts and console; pf state expressions; Equality, truthiness and coercion. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: JavaScript number uses IEEE-754 binary floating point, so safe integers and decimal fractions have finite precision limits. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: NaN is unequal to itself, -0 exists, and bigint is a separate exact-integer type. This draft therefore rejects: All decimal arithmetic is exact because number displays familiar decimal text.

includes: Handle finite checks, safe integers, floating approximation and bigint boundaries.
Implement and test numeric validation without relying on misleading NaN comparisons.
excludes: NaN is unequal to itself, -0 exists, and bigint is a separate exact-integer type.

outcomes: Implement and test numeric validation without relying on misleading NaN comparisons.

examples.title: Numbers, NaN and precision: concrete case
examples.situation: 0.1 + 0.2 is compared with a tolerance instead of exact equality to 0.3.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: 0.1 + 0.2 is compared with a tolerance instead of exact equality to 0.3.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Binary floating-point decimals can round; use tolerance or domain-appropriate decimal representation.
examples.result: The result follows because Binary floating-point decimals can round; use tolerance or domain-appropriate decimal representation.
